root.narration: The class birthday cake was a full sheet cake at 8 AM, but by the 2 PM party it is missing a big corner piece! Nobody admits to eating it. You decide to work BACKWARDS from the current state. The cake is in the cafeteria fridge now with a corner missing. At 1 PM the lunch staff saw it was still whole. At noon the teacher checked it and it was fine. At 11 AM a student helper moved it from the kitchen to the cafeteria fridge. So the cake was eaten between 1 PM and 2 PM. Who had access to the cafeteria fridge between 1 and 2 PM?

choices.text: Work backwards: cake was whole at 1 PM, missing a piece at 2 PM. Find out who accessed the cafeteria fridge during that one-hour window. That is your suspect list.
Investigate everyone who touched the cake in the morning since they knew where it was.
Ask every kid in the class if they ate cake.

outcome.headline: Working Backwards Pinpoints the Window!
outcome.explanation: By working backwards from the result (missing cake piece at 2 PM) through each checkpoint (whole at 1 PM, whole at noon, whole at 11 AM), you identified a one-hour window: 1 PM to 2 PM. Only two students had cafeteria passes during that time -- and one of them had frosting on their sleeve! Working backwards turned a wide-open mystery into a focused investigation with a tight time window and a short suspect list.
outcome.whatToRemember: Working backwards means starting with what happened and tracing back through each step to find when and where things changed. It narrows your search to the exact window of time.

keyTerms.definition: Starting with the result and tracing back step by step to figure out when and how it happened.
The specific period of time when something could have happened, based on when things were last checked.

outcome.headline: Wrong Time Period! Work Backwards!
outcome.explanation: Checking the morning makes sense since those people knew about the cake. But the cake was still whole at 1 PM! That means nobody touched it in the morning. You wasted time investigating a time period when nothing happened. If you had worked backwards from 2 PM, you would have immediately focused on the 1-2 PM window -- the only time the cake could have been eaten.
outcome.whatToRemember: Do not investigate every time period equally. Work backwards from when the problem was discovered to find the narrow window when it actually happened.

outcome.headline: Too Broad! Nobody Will Admit It!
outcome.explanation: Asking every kid in class wastes time and probably will not work since the cake-eater is unlikely to confess. You have checkpoint evidence showing the cake was whole at 1 PM and missing a piece at 2 PM. Working backwards gives you a one-hour window and a very short suspect list. Use your evidence to narrow the search instead of asking 25 kids the same question.
outcome.whatToRemember: When you have checkpoints (times when things were verified), use them! Work backwards to find the narrow window instead of casting a wide net.
